text: ESLAVA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 18 m. SE of Pampeluna.
ESMANT, a town on the Zanguebar coast, near the mouth of the Mongallo, in S lat. 9° 44'.

ESMERALDA, a village of Venezuela, situated on the N shore of the Upper Orinoco, near the point where it is joined by the Cassiquiari, at the foot of the Duida mountain, in N lat. 3° 11'. It is surrounded by a beautiful plain.

ESMERALDAS, a town of Ecuador, situated on the river of the same name, 2 leagues from its mouth, and 5 leagues from Atacames.—The river has its rise in the mountains of Pasto, near Quito, flows NW, and falls into the Pacific in lat. 0° 58' N, after a course of 110 m. Its mouth is 970 yds. wide and of great depth, but is impeded by a bar extending from the N shore. Its principal affluents are the Vichi and the Tiaune.

ESMERALDAS (SERRA-DAS), a mountain-chain of Brazil, in the E part of the prov. of Minas-Geraes. It is connected with the E part of the Serra-do- Espinhaço, and runs in an ENE and WSW direction about 80 m. The San João, the Fando, and the Mocuri rivers have their sources upon it.

ESMERY-HALLON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Ham. Pop. 1,452. ESNE, ESNE'H, or ASNA, a town of Upper Egypt, on the W bank of the Nile, 28 m. S of Thebes, in N lat. 25° 17′ 38". The town has well-supplied markets, and manufactories of cotton goods and pottery; and forms an important entrepot in the trade with Sennaar. Olin supposes the pop. may amount to 5,000. The harbour which it once had upon the Nile is now in a ruinous condition; and E. is chiefly remarkable for the ruins of the ancient Latopolis, of which it occupies the site. There is, in particular, in the most crowded part of the town, surrounded and partially concealed by mud cottages, the portico of a temple which, though almost buried beneath rubbish and the mud of the Nile, appeared to Denon the most perfect monument of ancient architecture which he had seen. Hamilton remarks that there is a great variety in the sculptures upon this portico, and is inclined, from the rude manner in which they are executed, to ascribe to them a very remote antiquity. Champollion, however, regards it as of comparatively modern date, and not earlier than the emperor Claudius. On the ceiling of the portico is painted a zodiac on which fanciful interpreters have founded singular reveries; and a profusion of sculpture adorns the walls, columns, architraves, and plinths.
ESNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Clary. Pop. 1,315.
ESNEUX, a commune and village of Belgium, in long. 121° 47′ 14". It is upwards of 5 leagues across, the prov. and 9 m. S of Liege, on the Ourte river. Pop. 1,522.
ESO, a small island of Dalmatia, 8 m. SW of Zara, between Isola Grossa and Ugliano. It is 7 m. in length.
ESOPUS, a township in the state of New York, U.S., 7 m. S of Kingston. Pop. 1,939.
ESPADACINTA, a fortified town of Portugal, lakes near the shore.—Also a bay on the SE coast ESP the borders of Spain, 34 m. SW of Miranda-de- Douro.
ESPAGNAC. See ISPAGNAC.
ESPAIN (St.), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, 16 m. SSW of Tours. Pop. 2,128.

ESPALION, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron. -The arrond. comprises the 9 cantons of Saint- Amans, Saint-Chely-d'Aubrac, Entraigues, E., Estaing, Sainte-Geneviève, Saint-Geniez, Laguiole, and Mur-de-Barrez. It has an area of 152,595 hect. Pop. in 1841, 66,913; in 1846, 67,139.—The cant., comprising 5 com., had a pop. of 11,280 in 1841.— The com., 16 m. NNE of Rhodez, had a pop. of 4,274 in 1846.
ESPALMADOR, an islet of the Mediterranean, in the Balearic group, in N lat. 38° 46'. It has a small port on its SW side.
ESPALY-SAINT-MARCEL, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haut-Loire, cant. of Le Puy. Pop. 1,131.
ESPAMISCACK, a lake of Lower Canada, 220 m. NE of Quebec, in N lat. 56°.
ESPARRAGOZA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 50 m. ESE of Lerida, on the l. bank of the Guadalema.
ESPARRAGUERA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 14 m. NW of Barcelona, on the river Llobregat. Pop. 2,366. It has cloth-factories and paper-mills.
ESPARTIL, or ESPARDELL, a small island in the Mediterranean, a little N of Formentera, in N lat. 38° 48'. It is 1¼ m. distant from the Isla-del-Espalmador.
ESPARTINAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 7 m. WNW of Seville.

ESPARZA, a settlement of Costa-Rica, on the coast of the Pacific, near the mouth of a small river which discharges itself into the E side of the gulf of Nicoya, in N lat. 10° 1′, and 10 m. N of Calderas.— Also a town of Spain, in the prov. and 28 m. ENE of Pampeluna, on the l. bank of the Salazar.
ESPAYRAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Entraigues. Pop. 2,132.
ESPE'DAILLAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Livernon. Pop. 1,066.
ESPEJA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. WNW of Soria. Pop. 2,000.—Also a town of Spain, in the prov. of Salamanca, 9 m. W of Cuidad- Rodrigo. Pop. 2,200.
ESPEJO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Cordova, 17 m. NNE of Montilla. Pop. 5,284.
ESPELETTE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrenees, 9 m. S of Bayonne. Pop. of com., 1,500.—The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. 8,886.
ESPELUY, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 14 m. NNW of Jaen, near the Guadalquiver.
ESPERA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 34 m. NE of Cadiz, on the Guadalete. Pop. 1,577.

ESPERANCE BAY, a bay on the S coast of Australia, in Nuytz land, in S lat. 33° 55' 07", E and nearly 4 leagues in depth, but is quite crowded with rocky islets. It has Cape-le-Grand on the E, and Observatory-island on the W. Sharks of enormous size are seen in the bay, and considerable abundance of other fish. The coast is barren, consisting chiefly of sand-hills with low vegetation; mountains appear in the interior, and there are saltof Van-Diemen's Land, 61 m. NNE from Musclebay. Its entrance is 14 m. wide, and it has a depth of 4 m.
ESPERANZA, an island of Patagonia, between Sarmiento and San-Estevan channels.

ESPERANZA INLET, a channel on the W coast of Vancouver island, having its mouth in N lat. 49° 48'. It stretches E by N 30 m.; and then turns S by W, to Nootka-sound, which it enters on the W side, thus insulating Nootka-island from the main. In its reach along the N side of Nootka island, it sends off two arms, each of about 10 m. in length, called Espinosa arm, and Zeballos arm; and opposite the NE corner of the island sends off a third arm called Macuina basin.
ESPERAUSSES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Lacaune. Pop. 1,072.
ESPERAZA, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, 8 m. S of Limoux. Pop. 1,403.
ESPERSHIELDS, a township in the p. of Bywell, Northumberland, 7 m. SW of Bywell. Pop. 198.

ESPICHEL (CAPE), a promontory on the W coast of Portugal, 21 m. SW of Lisbon, in N lat. 38° 24', W long. 9° 13'. It has a lighthouse upon it with a light 627 ft. above sea-level. It was the Barbarium promontorium of the ancients.
ESPIEDS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Meun-sur-Loire. Pop. 1,075.
ESPIEL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. NW of Cordova, near the Guadiato river. Pop. 1,088.

ESPIERRE, a town of Belgium, in W. Flanders, in the dep. of Jemappe, 8 m. SSE of Courtrai.—On the 22d May, 1794, the French attacked the English and Austrians at this place, and after an obstinate engagement were repulsed.
ESPINAR (EL), a village of Spain, in the prov. and 20 m. SW of Segovia.

ESPINHAÇO (SERRA-DO), the name given by some geographers to that main ridge in the Brazilian mountain-system which extends from the prov. of Minas-Geraes N into that of Bahia and Pernambuco, and S as far as San Paulo and Rio Grande. It is linked to the Serra-do-Mar, on the N, by the Serrada-Montequeira; and the Serra-dos-Vertentes extends from its S extremity to the Campos-de-Parecis: so that this grand mountain-system may be traced between 14° and 20° S lat., across the Pampas up to the foot of the Andes.
ESPINOSA-DE-HENARES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 22 m. NNE of Guadalaxara, near the l. bank of the Henares.
ESPINOSA-DEL-MONTE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. WSW of Logrono.
ESPINOSA-DE-LOS-MONTEROS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. W of Burgos, near the 1. bank of the Trueba, at an alt. of 1,273 ft. above sea-level.
ESPINOSA-DE-LA-RIBEIRA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 14 m. WNW of Leon, on the 1. bank of the Luna.
ESPIRAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Vertaison. Pop. 1,182.

ESPIRITO-SANTO, a small maritime province of Brazil, extending between 18° 30' and 21° 20' S 1at. It comprises an area of 38,442 sq. m.; and is bounded on the N by the prov. of Bahia, on the E by the Atlantic, along which it presents a coast line of about 150 m.; on the S it is divided by the Cabapuana or Camapoan, from the prov. of Rio-de- Janeiro; its W boundary is formed by the Cordilheira-dos-Aimores, which separates it from the prov. of Minas-Geraes. Pop. in 1812, 24,000, of whom 12,000 were slaves; it is now supposed to approximate to 60,000. This prov. is intersected by several mountain ranges, the principal of which are the Serra * ESP Guarapasi towards the SW, the Pero-cão to the N of the former, the Monte-Moreno on the S side of the bay of Espirito-Santo, the Mestre-Alvaro, a circular volcanic mountain to the N of the bay, and Monte-Agha. The principal rivers are the Carahipe or Doce, the Camapoan, Benevente or Reritigba, the Guandu, the Guarapari, the Itapi-Mirim, the Jecu or Rio-dos-Reis-Magos, which is connected by the Camboapina canal with the bay of E. S. and the Santo Maria. The estuaries of several of these rivers form safe and commodious harbours. The climate is mild and salubrious, and the soil in the valleys is extremely fertile, producing coffee, sugar, manioc, cacao, drugs, and cotton in abundance. The forests, which still cover a great portion of the prov., furnish, besides excellent timber, varieties of resinous trees, and of the fine woods used in cabinet work. and dve-woods. A kind of cotton cloth is extensively manufactured by the natives.—The prov. of E. S. was conquered in 1534 by Vasco-Fernandes- Continho, and in acknowledgment of his services in the East, was conferred upon that warrior by the Portuguese sovereign D. João III. The modern cap. is Nossa-Senhora-da-Victoria. - Also a mountainrange in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, running NNE between the Guaicuhi or Velhas and the São Francisco, to the confluence of those rivers.-Also a bay on the E coast of Brazil, in the prov. of the same name, in S lat. 20° 20'. It is of semicircular form, 9 m. in length and 6 in breadth, and possesses depth sufficient for vessels of the largest size. Its entrance, which lies between the extremity of Monte-Moreno and Punta-Pirahé, is about 3 m. in width, and is defended by several forts. The Santa-Maria and several other rivers flow into this bay. It encloses two islands, on one of which is the capital of the prov., Nossa-Senhora-da-Victoria. See also Santa MARIA.

ESPIRITO-SANTO, or VILLA-VELHAS, a maritime town of Brazil, formerly capital of the prov. of Espirito-Santo, situated on the S side of the bay of the same name, at the foot of Monte-Moreno. Its inhabitants, about 1,000 in number, find their chief employment in fishing. The houses are ill-built, and are thatched with straw. This town was founded by Vasco-Fernandes-Continho in 1535.

ESPIRITO-SANTO-DA-CRUZ-ALTA, a district and town of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedrodo-Rio-Grande. The district is separated from the prov. of Santa-Catharina, on the N, by the Uruguay. Pop., chiefly agricultural, 4,000, of whom 200 are Indians.

ESPIRITU-SANCTO, or SANCTI-SPIRITUS, a town of Cuba, capital of the jurisdiction of the same name, in the departemento-del-Centro, near Puerto- Principe, and 20 m. ESE of Havana. Pop. in 1841. 9,484, of whom 5,296 are whites, 2,722 free-coloured. and 1,466 slaves. The jurisdiction comprises 2 towns and 5 villages, and contained in 1841, 33,711 inhabitants.

ESPIRITU-SANTO, or TIERRA-AUSTRAL-DELESPIRITU-SANTO, an island of the South Pacific ocean, the largest and most westerly of the group of the New Hebrides, in S lat. 15°, and E long. 167°. It is 66 m. in its greatest length from N to S, and about 30 m. in breadth. It is generally mountainous, and on the W extremely precipitous. Excepting in the most elevated parts it is fertile and well-wooded. On the N coast are the bays of S. Felipe and S. Jago, both of which afford good harbourage. This island was discovered in 1606 by Quiros.
ESPIRITU-SANTO (CAPE), a headland on the NE coast of the island of Samar, in the Asiatic archipelago, in N lat. 12° 30′, and E long. 125° 30′.

ESPIRITU-SANTO (CAPE), a headland of Eastern Tierra-del-Fuego, on the NE coast, near the entrance of the strait of Magalhaens, in S lat. 52° 38', and W long. 68° 37'.—Also a group of islets in the Bahama archipelago, to the S of Andros island, in N lat. 24° 5', and W long. 77° 50'.—Also an island in the gulf of California, NNW of Cape S. Lorenzo, shore. in N lat. 24° 32′, and W long. 110° 45′. It is 15 m. in length, and about 6 m. in breadth.
ESPIRITU-SANTO, or SANCTI-SPIRITUS-Y-ZARZACAPILLA, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. of Badajoz, partido of Puebla-de-Alcoser, 50 m. ENE of Llerena, on the l. bank of the Guadalema.

ESPIRITU-SANTO BAY, or Lake San Jose, an extensive indentation of the coast of Texas, running up between the districts of Refugio and Victoria, in N lat. 28° 20', and W long. 97° 30'. On the SW side is Matagorda island, by which it is enclosed; on the SE it opens by an inlet of the same name into the gulf of Mexico. On the S it communicates with Aransas bay. It receives the Guadalupe and some other minor streams. It is of great extent, and forms an excellent harbour; though no towns have yet been founded upon its shores. This bay was originally named St. Louis by the French.

ESPIRITU-SANTO, or TAMPA BAY, an extensive indentation of the W coast of Florida, and the largest in the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 27° 40', and W long. 82° 45'. It is 40 m. long, and at its greatest breadth 35 m. across, with from 15 to 20 ft. of water on the bar. It is easy of access, and affords safe anchorage to any number of vessels. On the E is Hillsboro bay, which forms a spacious basin, and receives a river of the same name. At the N extremity is little Tampa bay. Several rivers of considerable magnitude flow into the bay to the S of the estuary of the Hillsboro.
ESPLAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariège, cant. of Saint-Girons. Pop. 1,485.
ESPORLES, a town on the W coast of the island of Majorca, 5 m. WNW of Palma. Pop. 1,500.

ESPOSENDA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Minho, 7 m. W of Barcelles, near the mouth of the Cavado. It has a harbour for small ships opposite Fao. Pop. 1,100.—Also a town of Spain, in Galicia, 12 m. SW of Orenes.

ESPRIT (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Landes, arrond. of Dax .- The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. of 15,612 in 1841.—The com. and town is situated on the r. bank of the Adour, opposite Bayonne, of which it forms a suburb. Pop. 7,324.
ESPRIT (SAINT), a small river of Lower Canada, which, after a winding and W course of about 20 m., falls into the river Assumption.
ESPRONCEDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 32 m. WSW of Pampeluna.

ESQUAMINES, three small islets in the bay of Mille Vaches, on the N coast of the gulf of St. Law- ESQUE/BERDES, a commune and village of rence. France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Lombres, on the r. bank of the Aa. Pop. 742.
ESQUEBIEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Pont-Croix. Pop. 1,599.
ESQUELBECQ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Wormhoudt, 11 m. SSE of Dunkirk. Pop. 1,804.
ESQUERIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Nuvion. Pop. 2,448.
ESQUERMES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Lille. Pop. 2,181. It has bleachfields, paper-mills, and chemical works.
ESQUILLADO (L'), a small island of the Mediterranean, near the coast of France, in N lat. 43° 3', E long. 6° 36'. ESQ

ESQUIMAULT HARBOUR, a port on the SE coast of Vancouver island, in N lat. 48° 27', W long. 123° 27', on the N side of Juan-de-Fuca strait, and 4 m. to the W of Victoria harbour and settlement. It is completely land-locked, and "smooth as a millpond," with a good anchorage for large ships close to

ESQUIMAUX, a race scattered along the coasts of Labrador and Hudson's bay, and the Arctic shores N and NW of Hudson's bay, as far as Behring's straits, and from thence to Cook's inlet and the peninsula of Alashka, entirely dissimilar in manners and character to the other Indian tribes. They are generally located to the N of the parallel of 68°, but are met with as far S as 50° N. The name, which has been given them by the Indians, signifies 'eaters of raw flesh.' Their colour is not that of copper, but approaches the tawny brown which distinguishes the inhabitants of the more northern parts of Europe; they have beards, and some of them have been observed with hair of different colours, in some fair, in others red, though the prevailing colour is black. These marks, by which they are evidently distinguished from the Americans, have inclined several philosophers to believe that they are of European descent. Their red and fair hair, found in the N of Europe more frequently than in any other country of the world,-but above all, their language, which is a dialect of that spoken in E. Greenland, the inhabitants of which are believed to have emigrated from Europe,—give to this conjecture a considerable appearance of probability. Their eyes are in general black, and they have broad lozenge-formed faces. The E. are dwarfish in size, and more bold and crafty than the Indians: but they use their women better, and display a higher degree of intelligence. They occupy the shores of the Polar sea, and live chiefly by catching seals and whales,-an operation which requires them to combine in large parties, and to this circumstance Dr. Richardson attributes the superior capacity they display for civilization. "It is a singular fact that tribes of this description, agreeing in form, features, and manners, and apparently of kindred race, occupy the whole shores of the Polar sea in Europe, Asia, and America. One would almost suppose that this variety of the human species had been created expressly to tenant those frozen regions to which their mode of life appears to attach them, as the Negro seems adapted by an opposite organization to the scorching heats of the torrid zone." There is in fact little variety of type, either in physical conformation, manners, or language, among the E., though scattered over an immense line of coast. Franklin, however, informs us that the E. tribes to the W of the meridian of 140° have at first a considerable difficulty in understanding the speech of those living to the E of that line.— The E. seen by those who have visited Hudson's straits, are always clothed in skins. They have boots or stockings reaching the knees; and breeches loose at the knees, and drawn round the waist with strings, in the form of a purse. They wear a kind of coat, which is drawn on like a shirt, and has a hood to cover the head, and two flaps, one before and another behind, descending considerably lower than the border of the other parts of the garment. The seams of these garments are neatly and strongly sewed with the dried sinews of deer and other animals, and their borders ornamented with stripes of the most beautiful furs. The dress of the women is little different from that of the men; their hoods are somewhat larger, the flaps of their upper garments before and behind descend farther, and their boots are wider, and extended with whalebone, so that, on some occasions, they are used as cradles for their children.—Their canoes are of two kinds: the larger sometimes capable of carrying upwards of 40 persons, -the smaller seldom carrying more than one. The small canoe is extremely long in proportion to its breadth; and both ends, which are a little raised, terminate in a sharp point. Its frame consists of slender ribs, supported by spars, the whole length of the canoe, and fastened with bandages of split whalebone; above and below, it is covered with skins dressed into a kind of parchment, and sewed tightly to the frame, leaving only one circular hole, equally distant from the two ends, just sufficient to receive one person, and fitted with a kind of hoop to tie round the waist, to keep him firm in his seat, and prevent the water from entering. Seated in this manner with his paddle, which has a blade at both ends, and with which he strikes on each side alternately, he proceeds at a rate not less than 7 or 8 m. an hour. Harpoons and other fishing instruments are secured upon the upper side of the canoe with straps of leather. The large canoes are in shape more like our boats; their length is generally four times their greatest breadth, and the stem is somewhat more pointed than the stern, which is round. Like the smaller canoes these are composed of ribs, held together by long spars, and covered with skins, which are sewed to the upper spar on the ledge, and leave the whole upper part open. They are called women's boats, because they are not navigated by men, and are used only for transporting their families and effects from place to place. They have no seats, so that those who row them must stand.

ESQUIMAUX BAY, a bay on the S coast of Labrador, discovered by Davis in 1586, but first named by the French. Since 1785 it has been resorted to for the purposes of trade by merchants of Quebec. In 1837 its mercantile establishments fell into the hands of the Hudson's Bay company, who kets, guns, ammunition, and provisions, for seal-oil, salmon, cod-fish, and furs. The trade with the Esquimaux is principally carried on at Kigolet, about 50 m. from the sea, for the natives have been gradually retiring from the bay as the seal-fishery declined. There are, however, a considerable number of mountaineer Indians and planters at the head of the bay, and two small cod-fishing establishments at its entrance, in N lat. 45° 30'.
ESQUIMAUX (CAPE), a cape in Hudson's bay, in N lat. 61° 12', W long. 94° 50'.

ESQUIMAUX ISLAND, an island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, near the S coast of Labrador, in the mouth of a bay of the same name, in N lat. 50° 15'. It is 7 or 8 m. in circumf.—Also an island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, in the Mingan group. It is 23 m. long.

ESQUIMAUX LAKE, a large sheet of brackish water in Arctic America, to the E of the Rein-deer mountains, and approaching within 60 m. of the bend of Mackenzie's river at Fort Good Hope. It has a large outlet into Liverpool bay, to the W of Cape Bathurst.

ESQUIPULAS, a town of Guatimala, in the dep. of Chiquimula, in N lat. 14° 56', 20 m. ESE of Chiquimula, on the dividing ridge between the basin of the Motagua on the NW, and that of the Lempa on the SE. Pop. 1,800. An annual fair is held here for 3 days, beginning on the 15th of January, which draws together a large concourse of people; most of whom, however, appear ostensibly at least as pilgrims to a celebrated image of the Crucifixion which is exhibited here in a handsome edifice about 1 m. from the town.
ESQUIULE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Saint-Marie-d'Oleron. Pop. 1,382.
ESRIEH, a town of Syria, 65 m. SSE of Aleppo, on the route to Tadmor.
ESROM, a town of Denmark, in Sieland, 30 m. N of Copenhagen. Pop. 1,000.
ESSAI, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Mesle-sur-Sarthe. Pop. 721.
ESSAID (KASR), a village of Upper Egypt, on the r. bank of the Nile, 25 m. ESE of Achmin.
ESSAROIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Recey-sur-Ource. Pop. 396.
ESSARTS (Les), a canton and small town of France, in the dep. of La Vendée.—The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. 11,107. The town is 9 m. NE of Bourbon-Vendée. Pop. 2,192.
ESSCHEN, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. and 20 m. N of Antwerp. Pop. 2,040.

ESSE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Illeet-Vilaine, cant. of Rhetiers. Pop. 1,703.—Also a river of France, an affluent of the Moselle, rising near Jouy, and having a course of about 30 m. ESSEK, ESZEK, ESSEGG, or OSSICK, the chief town of the gespanschaft of Verocze, and the cap. of Austrian Slavonia, situated in a level and marshy district, on the r. bank of the Drave, 13 m. above its efflux into the Danube, 80 m. NW of Belgrade, in N lat. 45° 34′ 13″, E long. 18° 42′ 5″. Its site has been identified with that of the Mursia or Mursa of the Romans, founded in 125 by Hadrian. It consists of four quarters; the upper town, the lower town, the new town, and the fortress. The military portion of the town, begun under the emperor Leopold I. in 1712, and finished in 1719, is well-built, and contains an arsenal and barracks capable, it is said, of containing 30,000 men; it is regularly fortified, and has an esplanade running round it. To the NW of it stands the upper town, which is approached ESS have 15 or 20 traders here, who barter cloth, blan- | is the residence of the merchants and dealers, and has well-attended fairs. To the SE of the fortress lies the lower town, the site of the ancient Mursa, consisting of broad and handsome streets. The new town is composed rather of farms and gardens than of lines of streets. The fortress and suburbs contain altogether about 1,800 houses, 5 Roman Catholic churches, 4 chapels, and a Greek church. The pop., chiefly of German descent, amounts to 11,200. The town-hall and the house-of-assembly are handsome edifices. There are a high-school, a gymnasium, a Greek school, a military academy, and Franciscan and Capuchin monasteries, within the town. A causeway and wooden bridge together 21 m. long, 55 ft. broad, and 9 ft. high, constructed in 1712, leads across the Drave and the swamps on its N bank, into the Hungarian com. of Baranya. With the exception of silk-spinning, and some manufactories of silk stuffs, there is little mechanical industry in this town; but it has a considerable trade in grain, horses, cattle, and raw hides; chiefly conducted during its fairs, of which 4 are held in the year.

ESSEN, a town of Rhenish Prussia, in the circle and 18 m. NE of Düsseldorf, on the Berne, in N lat. 51° 27′, E long. 7° 2′. Pop. 5,784 in 1837. It is surrounded by walls, and is the seat of the mining and crown-domain boards. It has 2 Protestant, and 2 Catholic churches, a Capuchin monastery, an orphan-asylum, a gymnasium with 11 teachers, and an hospital. Its manufactures consist of woollen and linen goods, leather, fire-arms, iron and steel ware, gas-apparatus, and vitriol. The town has some trade; and there are coal-mines in the vicinity as well as iron-works.
ESSEN, a village of Hanover, 15 m. NE of Osnabruck.
ESSENDIN, a parish of Hertfordshire, 3 m. E of Hatfield. Area 2,170 acres. Pop. 690.
ESSENDINE, a parish in Rutlandshire, 4 m. NE of Stamford. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 152.
ESSENHEIM, a village of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the Lower Palatinate, on the Selze. Pop. 1,035.
ESSENO, a territory described by Portuguese writers as situated on the upper part of the river Zaire, in the kingdom of Congo. It contains a town of the same name.

ESSEQUIBO, a county or province of British Guayana, comprising the regions drained by the affluents of the great river of the same name. It is now united with Demerara; and its statistics, as blended with those of the latter co., will be found under the article DEMERARA. The W frontiers of E. run from Punta Barima in W long. 6° 6', along the Cuyuni; thence crossing the Sierra Imataca to the source of the Parawanjaura; thence along that river to its influx into the Cuyuni; thence up the Aruarua, an affluent of the Cuyuni on the r. bank, and from the sources of that river, along the Irutibuh range, till it strikes the dividing ridge betwixt the head-streams of the Carony flowing W, and those of the Mazaruni flowing E, nearly under the parallel of 5° 30' N, and the meridian of 61° W. From this point, which is the extreme W point of the colony, the boundary runs in an irregular but prevailingly SE course till it strikes the Xuruma river, and proceeds up that river to its source, whence it runs along the dividing ridge between the head-streams of the Parima on the SW, and those of the Cuyuwini and the Yuawauri, and other head-streams of the Essequibo, on the NE, till it strikes the Sierra Acarai, on the S of the meridian of 1° S, whence the S boundary-line of the colony is drawn in a direction to the N of E, till it strikes the sources of the from the fortress by an avenue 1,100 paces long. It | Corentyn. Nearly the whole of the vast extent of country comprised between the line just traced on the Wand S, and the l. bank of the Essequibo river, is a rich but uncultivated waste, inhabited only by small scattered bands of Indians. The Essequibo, the largest river of Guayana, has its origin in the Sierra Acarai, in 0° 40' N, and 58° 36' W long. The first portion of its course is in a NE direction through a rich mountain-valley; and its early tributaries are the Caneruau and the Wapuau from the SE, and the Camoa from the SW. Having passed the Ouangou mountains, or N angle of the Sierra Acarai, it flows N to the confluence of the Cassi-Kityou or Yuawauri, which has its sources near those of the Rio-Branco or Parima. From this point, its course is NE to the junction of the Cuyuvini, under the parallel of 2° 16' N; whence it pursues a NW course, much impeded with cataracts, throughout a distance of 70 m. Under the parallel of 4° N, it receives the Rupununi, a river which has a N and NE course of about 250 m., mostly through wide savannahs. From this point of junction, the E. continues to flow in a NW direction, and in 4° 46' N receives the Siparuni from the SW. Its course below this point is again much impeded by rapids and islands; but, after receiving the Potaro, it has a pretty direct course N, between its future tributary the Mazaruni flowing in a parallel course 12 m. to the W, and the Demerara river flowing likewise in a parallel course 15 m. to the E. About 50 m. above its mouth, a series of rapids occur which prevent the tide ascending further, and terminate the ship-navigation of the river. About 6 m. NW of Saccaro, it receives the united tribute of the Mazaruni and the Cuyuni, in a noble stream about 1 m. wide at its mouth. Continuing to flow N, the E. gradually widens, until its estuary attains a breadth of nearly 20 m. Its seaward entrance is however much impeded by shoals and sand-banks. Its entire course is about 640 m. Its current varies with the season; but soon after the rains is seldom more than 4 knots per hour, except in the rapids.—At the mouth of this great river, lie the three islands of Leguan, Waakenaam, and Tiger, which are cultivated like gardens, and produce luxuriant crops of coffee and sugar. The Dutch, in former times, had cotton, indigo, and cocoa plantations up the E., and even beyond their old cap. of Kykoveral, at the fork or junction of the Mazaruni; but all traces of these clearings are now lost in the vast dense forest.
ESSERTINES, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district of Echallens. Pop. 619.

ESSEX, a maritime county on the E coast of England; bounded on the N by Suffolk, from which it is divided by the river Stour, and by Cambridgeshire; on the W by Hertfordshire and Middlesex, from which it is separated by the rivers Stort and Lea; on the S by Kent, from which it is separated by the Thames; and on the E by the German ocean. It is of an irregular four-sided form, about 225 m. in circumf.; 60 m. in extent from E to W; and 50 m. from N to S. Area 979,000 acres. According to the ordnance map it contains only 942,720 acres: its area is estimated, in the highway returns, at 1,533 sq. m.; in the returns of poor rates at 976,000 acres. About 15,000 acres, including forests, are reckoned waste lands.—In the German ocean, and partly in the Thames, are a number of islands belonging to this co. Of these the island of Mersea, or Mersey, is the most valuable: it lies in the estuary or bay formed between the rivers Colne and Blackwater, about 10 m. S of Colchester. Those of Foulness, Wallasea, Potten, Havengore, and New-England, are contiguous to each other, and form a kind of broken promontory in the German ocean between the rivers Crouch and Thames. Canvey is situated to the SW of these, near the mouth of the Thames. Among the minor islands are Horsey on the NE coast, and Northey and Osey at the mouth of the Blackwater. It is from the violent action of the sea that the coasts of E. have been thus broken into islands, promontories, and peninsulas. The coast is so irregular and broken that it is difficult to estimate the exact length of it; but without reckoning the estuaries, its length is about 105 m. from the mouth of the Lea in the Thames, to the mouth of the Stour at the port of Harwich. The coast, except to a trifling extent at Harwich, Southend, and Purfleet, is protected from further invasions of the sea by strong embankments, by which extensive and valuable salt-marshes have been retained. Physical aspect, climate, &c.] This co. composes part of that tract of country on the E side of England which forms the largest connected space of level ground in the whole island: not one lofty eminence or rocky ridge being found here in several contiguous counties. The surface of E. is not, however, totally flat; but is diversified by gentle hills and dales; and towards the NW, whence most of its rivers proceed, the face of the country rises and presents a continued inequality of surface. Extensive salt-marshes border most of the coast; the banks of the Thames, and the lower parts near the sea, are likewise low and marshy. The hundreds of E., on the S coast, are proverbially unhealthy from their being exposed to the E winds and sea-fogs, which, together with the unwholesome exhalations from the marshes, and constant dampness, give rise to intermittent fevers and other diseases; but the middle and N districts of the co. enjoy a wholesome, clear air, and a fine dry soil. The most extensive view in E. is from the brow of Langdon, which is believed to present the finest prospect in England. "The ascent, on the N side of this eminence," observes Mr. Wright, in his history of E., published in 1836, "is gradual and easy; but on the SSE, and SW, the traveller is astonished at the descent before him, which exhibits a very beautiful and extensive scene, with London to the r., the Thames winding through the vale, and to the l. the river Medway." Young, in his Southern Tour, says of this panoramic scene, "I never beheld anything equal to it in the West of England,—that region of landscape!" Geological character.] Few cos. possess less minerals than E., which is so nearly exempt from quarries, or any mass of rock, that the houses are almost wholly built of brick. The London clay extends nearly over all E. It has been pierced in various places, and found of great thickness. Geologists have distinguished the district which includes this co. as that in which the superior strata rest on chalk. Amongst these superstrata, besides the vegetable mould and the London clay, are found brick-earth, sand, crag, pipe-clay, and the "plastic clay formation." In the tertiary beds of E. are found not only the bones of elephants, but of the carnivora now inhabiting the hot regions of the earth; among them are the bones of the hyena, wolf, and tiger, associated with the remains of the hippopotamus, rhinoceros, deer, and ox.—Some mineral waters rise in this co., but few have obtained much repute. That of Tilbury is impregnated with some earthy and muriatic salts. Rivers.] Besides the Thames, the Stour, the Lea, and the Stort, which only bound this co., the principal rivers are the Colne, the Blackwater, the Chelmer, the Crouch, the Roding, the Ingerbourn, and the Cam.—The Colne rises near Redgwell, in the NW part of the co., and runs SE, passing in its course by Hedlingham, Halstead, the Colnes, and Colchester; and discharges itself into the sea, about 8 m. below Colchester, to the harbour of which town it is navigable for vessels about 150 tons burthen .- The Blackwater rises in the NW, near Saffron-Walden, and flows SE by Radwinter, Great-Bardfield, Bocking, Coggeshall, Kelvedon, and Wickham mills; and being joined by the Chelmer at Maldon, both together enter the wide estuary called Blackwater-bay.- The Chelmer rises also in the NW near Thaxted, and running SE by Dunmow, Waltham, Chelmsford, and Maldon, joins the Blackwater, the estuary of which extends 12 m. into the country, while the navigation has been extended to Chelmsford.—The Crouch flows from two springs in Little Burgsted and Landon, and runs E, passing Wickford, and Hullbridge, where it is navigable at high water: it then flows by Criksey and Burnham, to the sea at Foulness. It is tidal for 16 m.—The Roding rises in the central part of the co. near Dunmow; passing S through an extensive tract to which it gives the name of the Rodings, or Roothings, it turns to the W at Chipping- Ongar, in Epping-Forest; and again turning E near Wanstead, it flows through a level district, by Ilford, to which it has been rendered navigable, and Barking, to the Thames at Barking-reach near East Ham, 7 m. from London.—The Ingerbourn is a small stream rising to the SE of Stapleford-Abbots, and, winding S to Upminster, and thence to Rainham, falls into the Thames at Earith-reach.—The Cam is composed of two branches, one of which rises on the borders of Bedfordshire; the other, bearing the classic name of the Granta, rises at Quendon, and flowing N, hastens, with its sister-stream, to Cambridge.—All the E. rivers, except the Crouch and other minor streams, have their sources in the NW and highest part of the co. Soil and produce.] Every species of loam, light gravelly land, and a good share of meadow and marsh ground, occur in this co. On the E and S it is generally marshy with gravel intermixed; on the W the soils vary from light thin loam upon gravel to tough clay upon brick earth. In the district of the Rodings the soil is strong wet loam. In the N and central parts there are strong and moist soils, as well as light loam upon marl. The co. is on the whole fertile, and possesses some particularly fine land, although the preponderance of clay soils increases the expense of cultivation considerably. The greater part is enclosed, and rendered highly productive by skilful management; but in some districts the holdings are only from year to year. Corn-farming is the distinguishing feature of the district; but besides the usual crops, including turnips and potatoes, a considerable quantity of rape-seed is grown; mustard, rye-grass, tares, and trefoil are also produced. The cultivation of hops is on the increase: on an average of seven years to 1835, the number of acres annually devoted to this purpose was 378, and the quantity produced 177,174 lbs. The cultivation of carraway, coriander, and teazel, is peculiar to this co. These seeds are all sown together, in a kind of treble crop, but they come to maturity at different periods, and the succession of the whole crop lasts 3 or 4 years. Saffron, which was at one time cultivated in various parts of the kingdom, has been ultimately grown almost solely in a circuit of about 10 m. between Saffron-Walden and Cambridge. Potatoes are extensively reared for the London market; and cabbages are cultivated in almost every part of the co., as food for cattle, sheep, and pigs. In the marshy districts osiers are considered a profitable cultivation. The greater part of the co. is well-wooded: besides the timber growing in the hedge-rows, there are about 50,000 acres in wood and woodlands. In the reign of James II., the forest of Essex extended almost over all the co. There are now in the SW two forests, Epping and Hainault, near each other, but separated by the Roding. These together cover about 10,000 acres, and contain some noble trees.- Though this co. is not highly celebrated for its dairies, yet those in the p. of Epping and its vicinity have long been famous for the richness of their cream and butter. Pigs in great numbers are fattened for the London markets on the refuse of the dairies, and otherwise; and E. is proverbially distinguished for its calves, which have long been held in high estimation in the London market. The marsh lands are the principal grazing-districts. The stock of sheep has been estimated at 500,000. There is no peculiar breed of horses; but the Suffolk punches seem to be favourites. The farms and estates vary greatly in size; but there are few cos. containing so many small sized farms occupied by their proprietors. Trade and manufactures.] E. derives many advantages from its maritime trade, as well as from its vicinity to the metropolis. The convenience of its water-carriage, and the goodness of its roads, have hitherto given it a commercial superiority over many other cos. Being peculiarly an agricultural co., its trade consists principally in its vegetable and animal produce.—Its celebrated oyster-fisheries must also be noticed as a branch of trade of some importance. The fisheries of E. are very productive of oysters: above 160 decked boats, from 8 to 50 tons burden, are almost always at work; and vessels come from Kent to purchase oysters. The principal breeding rivers are the Crouch—by far the most certain in produce—the Blackwater, and the Colne. The beds or layings are in the adjoining creeks; and from these the stock is supplied to other oyster-beds. It is calculated that not less than 15,000 bushels are supplied in a season; and the capital employed in this trade is stated to be from 60 to £80,000. The oysters are taken to London, Hamburgh, Bremen, Holland, France, and Flanders. Some of the inhabitants are employed in other fisheries, and in taking ducks, dun birds, &c., which are very numerous in Mersey isle and elsewhere.—The woollen manufacture was formerly of some importance in E., but has continued to decline, and has now ceased to be of any importance. In 1838 there was only one woollen mill in the co. Silk manufactures of different kinds are carried on in several towns. In 1838 there were 7 silk mills, employing 1,441 hands. Strawplait manufacture is conducted on a large scale in and around Castle-Hedingham. Canal and inland navigation.] In a co. termed, with propriety, maritime, it could not be expected that much occasion would be found for canals, even before the prospective or partial introduction of railways; yet some works of this kind have been constructed, communicating with the metropolis, and considerable river-navigations have been formed which have been highly beneficial to trade and commerce. The Stour is navigable to Sudbury, 30 m. above Harwich. The Thames is navigable throughout that part of its course which bounds this co.; and, -besides the navigation of the Colne, to Colchester; of the Blackwater, and Chelmer, to Chelmsford,—with a canal running from opposite Northey isle to Heybridge;—and of the Crouch, to Hull bridge for large vessels;—a canal navigation has been formed between the Thames, the Lea, and the Stort—though this may be said to belong more to Middlesex than to E.—whereby the Lea is made navigable throughout that part of its course which is connected with this county, and the Stort, from Bishop-Stortford to its junction with the Lea. A navigable canal from the former, along the valley of the Cam, to the navigable portion of that river near Cambridge, thus uniting the Thames and the Lea with the Ouse and the Cam, was projected and authorized by act of parliament, so long ago as 1812-14; but has not yet been accomplished. Roads.] The principal roads of this co. are those from London to Harwich and to Newmarket. They enter this co. together crossing the Lea river, at Stratford-le-Bow; proceeding to Stratford, the Newmarket road branches-off through Epping. The Harwich road runs through Romford, Brentwood, Chelmsford, Witham, and Colchester, by Manningtree to Harwich. From its comparatively even surface and the nature of its soils, E. is well adapted, with suitable materials, to the formation of good roads; and these advantages have been so well improved, that it may safely be asserted no better roads are to be found in England. The highway returns for 1839, show an expenditure of £31,669 on 3,113 m. of road in this co. Railways.] The Eastern Counties railway, projected in 1833, or 1834, was opened on 18th June 1839, from a temporary terminus at Devonshire-street, Mile-end, to Romford, 101 m.; and on 3d June, 1840, to Brentwood, a distance of 18 m. It has now been completed to Ipswich, and thence to Norwich; and has branches to Braintree and Maldon. A railway from Harwich joins it near Colchester. The Northern and Eastern railway from Cambridge to London was opened to Broxbourne in August 1840. It enters this co. by crossing the river Lea to Broxbourne, and running nearly in the line of the Newmarket-road, near to Bishop-Stortford, and by Newport, Littlebury, and Chesterford, quits E. near the junction of the Newmarket-road with the road from Cambridge. Civil and Ecclesiastical divisions, &c.] The county is politically divided into N. and S. Essex. Of 14 hundreds, 5 half-hundreds, and 1 royal liberty, into which it is subdivided, the northern division comprises the hundreds of Uttlesford, Huckford, Lexden, Tendring, Winstree, Thurstable, Witham, and Dunmow, and the half-hundreds of Clavering, and Freshwell; the southern, the hundreds of Ongar, Chafford, Barstable, Chelmsford, Dengie, and Rochford, the half hundreds of Harlow, Waltham, Becontree, and the royal liberty of Havering. The pop. of the hundreds and divisions of E. in 1831 and 1841 was as follows: Pop. in 1831. Pop. in 1841. Area. A considerable diminution took place in the rate of increase in the pop. of E. during the ten years between 1831 and 1841, as compared with the previous decades. In 1801 the pop. was 226,437; in 1811 it amounted to 252,473, being an increase of 11 per cent.; in 1821 there was an increase of 15 per cent., the numbers then being 289,424; in 1831 the numbers were 317,507, being an increase of 10 per cent.; while in the subsequent ten years, up to 1841, the increase was only at the rate of 8.6 per cent., the pop. of the co. then being 344,979, or about 4.4 per cent. below the average of the whole country. Of the pop., as returned in 1841, there were under 20 years of age, males 81,284, females 81,076; above 20 years of age, males 91,064, females 91,555. The number of persons born in the co. was 297,671; born elsewhere 47,308, of whom 2,216 were natives of Ireland, and 637 of Scotland. The number of inhabited houses was 67,618, which would give a proportion of about 5.1 persons to each house. - Each division sends 2 members to parliament. The polling-places in the N division are at Braintree, Tendring, Colchester, and Saffron-Walden, the principal place of election being at Braintree; those in the S division are at Chelmsford, Billericay, Romford, and Epping, the principal place of election being at Chelmsford. ESS The number of electors registered for the county, in 1837, was 11,446; in 1846, 10,887. In the northern divisions there are 2 boroughs, each returning 2 members, namely, Harwich, in Tendring hundred, and Colchester, in Lexden hundred; in the southern, 1 returning 2 members, namely, Maldon, in Dengie hundred. Essex is in the home-circuit: the assizes and quarter-sessions are held at Chelmsford, where the shire-hall and the old county-jail are situated. The new county-jail is at Springfield. The total value of assessed property in 1815 was £1,556,836; in 1843, £1,935,610. E. is in the dio. of London and prov. of Canterbury; but the ecclesiastical commissioners have proposed to transfer it, with the exception of several parishes, to the dio. of Rochester. It comprises the archd. of E. and Colchester, and part of that of Middlesex. There are 415 parishes and townships in the co., besides parts of 4 other parishes.—The poor rate returns for 3 years to Easter 1750, show an average expenditure of £38,233 on the poor of this county; for 1803, an expenditure of £184,428; for 1813, an expenditure of £325,219; for 1821, an expenditure of £288,921; for 1846-7 of £177,195; being at the rate of 2s. <math>1\frac{3}{4}</math>d. in the pound on the annual value of property assessed to poor's rate within the co. The rate per cent. of paupers relieved in 1846-7, on the estimated pop. of the co., was 14.2; the average for England and Wales being 8.8 per cent. In 1835, ern District, comprising the townships of Anderdon, Colchester, Gosfield, Maidstone, Mersea, Malden, Rochester, and Sandwich. It returns a member to the house-of-assembly.

ESSEX, a county of the state of Vermont, U. S., comprising an area of 225 sq. m., bounded on the E by Connecticut river, and watered by Nulhegan, Moose, and Clyde rivers, and by the head-waters of St. Francis river. Its surface is irregular, and the soil to a great extent poor and unproductive. Pop. in 1840, 4,226. Its cap. is Guildhall.—Also a county in the state of Massachusetts, containing a superficies of 360 sq. m., bounded on the N by New Hampshire, and on the E by the Atlantic. It is watered by Merrimac and Shawsheen rivers, and has several good harbours. The surface is broken and hilly, but the soil in many parts possesses considerable fertility. Pop. 94,987. Its chief towns are Salem, Newbury port, and Ipswich. It contains a township of the same name, 32 m. NE of Boston. Pop. 1,450 .- Also a county in the state of New York, comprising an area of 1,779 sq. m., bordered on the E by Lake Champlain. It is generally hilly. Towards the W is Mount Marcy, which rises 5,467 ft. above sea-level. Common and magnetic iron are found in large quantities throughout its entire extent, and especially in the Adirondac mountains, where extensive steel-works have been erected. Pop. 23,643. Its cap. is Elizabethtown. It contains a township of the same name, 138 m. N of Albany. It is watered by Bouquet river, an affluent of Lake Champlain, and in some parts has considerable fertility. Pop. 1,681.—Also a county in the state of New Jersey, containing an area of 241 sq. m., intersected by two mountain-chains, and drained by Passaic and Rahway rivers, and some minor streams. The soil is generally red shale. Pop. 44,621. Its capital is Newark.—Also a county of the state of Virginia, comprising an extent of 280 sq. m., bordered on the NE by the Rappahannock, and watered by the tributaries of that river. Its surface is undulating, but except on the streams, its soil, which is sandy, possesses little fertility. Pop. 11,309. Its capital is Tappahannock.—Also a township of Chittenden co., in the state of Vermont, 48 m. NW of Montpelier, bordered on the S by Onion river, and watered by Brown's river, Steven's brook, and other smaller streams. The soil in the N and E, consisting of sand and clay loam, has considerable fertility, and is covered with hardwood; in other parts pine forms the principal timber. Pop. 1,824.—Also a village of Saybrook township, Middlesex co., in the state of Connecticut, 37 m. SE of Hartford, on Connecticut river, 7 m. from its mouth. Pop. 1,000.
ESSEY, a town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Mele-sur-Sarthe, 11 m. NE of Alençon. Pop. 725. It has a parish-church, and a small hospital, and contains the ruins of a castle.
ESSEY-LES-PONTS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Chateau- Vilaine. Pop. 263. It affords good wine.
ESSEY-EN-VOIVRE, a town of France, in the dep. of the Meurthe, cant. of Thiaucourt, 19 m. N of Toul, on the Mad. Pop. 600.
ESSIE. See RHYNIE AND ESSIE.
ESSIE, or Eassie and Nevay, two parishes consolidated into one, on the W verge of Forfarshire, 7 m. WSW of Forfar. Area 5,120 English acres, or 8 sq. m. Pop. 732.
ESSIL. See Speymouth.
ESSING, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Bavaria, on the Altmuhl. Pop. 278.
ESSINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Jaxt, bail. and 4 m. SW of Aalen, on the Kems. Pop. 1,683.
ESSINGTON, a township in the p. of Bushbury, Staffordshire, 5 m. NE of Wolverhampton. Pop. 623.
ESSINGTON. See Port-Essington.

ESSINGTON (PORT), an inlet of British North America, on the W coast, to the E of Pitt's island, and at the N extremity of Grenville channel, in N lat. 54° 13'. It is enclosed on the N and E by lofty mountains covered with perpetual snow. It was discovered by Vancouver.

ESSLING, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, lower circle of Mannhartsberg, 8 m. E of Vienna, near a small arm of the Danube.—It is noted for the victory over the Austrians gained by the French which took place here on the 21st and 22d of May, 1809, and on which occasion Massena was created prince of Essling.

ESSLINGEN, a bailiwick and town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar. The town is 7 m. ESE of Stuttgard, on the r. bank of the Neckar, and on the railway to Ulm, at an alt. of 2,346 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 6,833. It is surrounded by old ruinous walls, flanked with towers, and has 5 suburbs. Of its ancient buildings the principal are the castle and the cathedral. It has also a church, a handsome EST town-house, a richly endowed hospital, an orphan's asylum, a lazaretto, a gymnasium, a polytechnical, a normal, and a classical academy, and a school for deaf mutes. It has extensive manufactories of cloth, gloves, lacquered ware, jewellery, and mathematical and physical instruments, a metal foundry, several cotton and wool-spinning-mills, and a paper and several fuller's mills. The trade consists chiefly in fruit and fruit-trees. The vineyards in the environs afford a very agreeable sparkling wine.-E. was formerly a free imperial town, and is of great antiquity. Under the Hohenstaufen it enjoyed great prosperity, and was often the residence of the emperors. It was united to Wurtemberg in 1802. The bail. of E. contains 20,620 inhabitants.
ESSOMES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Château-Thierry. Pop. 2,000. It has a considerable trade in grain.

ESSONNES, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of the Loiret, in the forest and 12 m. NE of Orleans; runs NE under the name of Œuf to Pithiviers-le-Vieil; thence takes the name of the Essonnes, runs E, then N, passing Malsherbes on the confines of the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, enters the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, waters La Ferté-Aleps and Essonne, and after a total course of 60 m., falls into the Seine at Corbeil. This river affords extensive water-power. Its chief affluent is the Juine .-- Also a commune and town in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise. cant. and 1 m. SW of Corbeil, on the river of the same name, and on the road from Paris to Fontainebleau. Pop. in 1841, 3,612. It has numerous corn, fulling, tan, and tobacco mills, several wool, silk, and cotton spinning mills, extensive manufactories of printed goods and table linen, a fine paper-mill, a powder mill, a manufactory of fire-arms, a copperfoundry, extensive lime-kilns, and several tanneries.
ESSOUAN. See Assuan.

ESSOYES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Aube, arrond. of Barsur-Seine. The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,097; in 1841, 13,292. The town is on the Ource, 12 m. SE of Bar-sur-Seine. Pop. in 1841, 1,727. It has 3 annual fairs.
ESTABL-ANTAR, or EL-ASTABEL, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Hedjaz, near the shore of the Red sea, 220 m. NW of Medina, and on the route of the caravan from Cairo to Mecca.
ESTABLES (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, cant. of Fay-le-Froid, 19 m. SE of Le-Puy. Pop. 1,044.
ESTABLIMENT, a town of the Balearic islands, in the island of Majorca, partido of Palma. Pop. 1,598.

ESTACA (Point), a headland on the coast of Galicia, forming the extreme N point of Spain. It is in N lat. 43° 47′ 50″, W long. 7° 38′ 26″, 13 m. W of Cape Vares, and 64 m. E of Cape Aquillon. It runs down in a rugged and irregular mass of rock to the sea, and has several small pointed rocks off it.
ESTADENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Aspet. Pop. 1,402.
ESTADILLA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. of Huesca, 8 m. E of Barbastro, near the 1. bank of the Cinca.
ESTAFORT. See ASTAFFORT.

ESTAGEL, a commune and small town of France, in the dep. of Pyrénées-Orientales, 12 m. WNW of Perpignan, on the r. bank of the Gly. Pop. 2,237. A large quantity of honey is collected in the environs of this place.
ESTAIMBOURG, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 7 m. N of Tournai. Pop. 1,056.
ESTAIMPIUS, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 8 m. NW of Tournai. | brandy. Pop. 6,000. In the neighbourhood is a Pop. 1,000.
ESTAIN. See ETAIN.

ESTAING, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Espalion. -The cant., comprising 5 com., had a pop. of 8,363 in 1841. The com. and town is situated 5 m. NW of Espalion, at the foot of the Viadène mountains. Pop. 1,375.
ESTAING BAY, an inlet on the W coast of Saghalien island, in N lat. 48° 57', E long. 142° 31'.
ESTAIRES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Melville, on the river Lys, 14 m. W of Lille. Pop. 6,825. It carries on some manufactures, chiefly in linen and soap.
ESTANCIA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 25 m. SW of Sergipe-del-Rey, on the Piauhy, an affluent of the Real. Pop. 3,000. virons.
ESTANG, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gers, 8 m. N of Nogaro. Pop. 1,174.
ESTAPA, or ISTAPA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Tabasco, 16 m. SW of Villa-Hermosa, on the r. bank of the Tabasco.
ESTARAC, an ancient division of the prov. of Guienne, in France. Its cap. was Mirande. Its area, amounting to 173,230 hect., is now divided between the deps. of Gers and Hautes-Pyrénées.
ESTARRONA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 4 m. W of Vittoria.
ESTAVAR, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Saillagouse. Pop. 327.
ESTAVAYER, a small well-built town in the Swiss cant. of Friburg, beautifully situated on an eminence near the E shore of the lake of Neufchatel, 13 m. W of Friburg. Pop. 1,381.
ESTAVILLO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 10 m. SSW of Vittoria.

ESTE, a walled town of Austrian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 16 m. SSW of Padua, in N lat. 45° 13' 21". It is pleasantly situated at the foot of the Euganean hills, near the conflux of the Bacchiglione and Gua, in a highly picturesque country. It contams several good buildings, and has manufactories of silk goods and earthenware. Pop. 8,850. It occupies the site of the Roman Ateste, and gives title to a princely house celebrated in history from the middle ages till our own days.—Also a river of Hanover, which falls into the Elbe, near Estebrügge, after a NW course of about 32 m.
ESTEBAN-DE-BAS (SAN), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. WNW of Gerona.
ESTEBAN - DE - GORMAZ (SAN), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 39 m. WSW of Soria, on the r. bank of the Duero.
ESTEBAN - DE - LITERA (SAN), a village of Spain, in the prov. of Huesca, 15 m. SE of Barbastro.
ESTEBAN-DEL-PUERTO (SAN), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 45 m. NE of Jaen.
ESTEBAN-DE-LA-SIERRA (SAN), a village of Spain, in the prov. and 36 m. S of Salamanca.
ESTEBAN - DEL - VALLE (SAN), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 32 m. SW of Avila.
ESTEBRUGGE, a village of Hanover, in the duchy of Bremen, 3 m. NNE of Buxtehude, on the L bank of the Este. Pop. 600.
ESTELAS, two islets off the W coast of Galicia, in Spain, at the entrance of the bay of Bayona.

ESTELLA, a town of Spain, in Navarre, in the prov. and 20 m. SW of Pampeluna, agreeably situated on the l. bank of the river Ega, at its junction with the Amescua, between Pampeluna and Viana, In a basin, the sides of which are formed by high and rocky mountains which entirely command the town. It has manufactories of woollen cloths, oil, and Buchon. EST beautiful Cistercian monastery.—E. was long the head-quarters of Don Carlos, who was proclaimed here in November 1833.
ESTENAZ, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, 27 m. NNW of Sataliah, and 36 m. SW of Isbarteh. It occupies the site of the ancient Termessus.
ESTEPA-LA-VIEJA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Andalusia, 15 m. S of Ecija, near the Genil. Pop. 6,559 in 1844.
ESTEPAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 9 m. WSW of Burgos, near the r. bank of the Arlanzon.

ESTEPHE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Nontron. Pop. 1,094. -Also a commune in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Pauillac, near the r. bank of the Gironde. Pop. 2,220. Wine is rather extensively grown in the en-

ESTEPONA, a town of Spain, on the coast of Grenada, 25 m. ENE of Gibraltar, and 16 m. SW of Marbella. Not many years since it was a mere fishing-village; but it is now a thriving town with 6,000 inhabitants, who carry on an active fishing and coasting trade. The environs are fruitful in oranges and lemons, which are largely exported to England.
ESTERABAD. See ASTERABAD.
ESTERBY, a village of Sweden, in the laen and 30 m. NNE of Upsala, near the celebrated Dannemora mine. It is a neat well-arranged place, though situated in a dreary district.
